researchNotes: First topic of Part A Unit III (History, Culture of India, and Indian National Movement — 10 questions total for the unit, denser than Units I/II's 5 questions each). Two real hits plus standard, well-established Indus Valley facts. A useful real-exam pattern emerged: TNPSC likes to test the geography/political-boundary angle (which sites are in Pakistan vs. India) and 'NOT all X is Y' generalization traps, not just bare dates/names. Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned the OCR bank with a tighter regex (the earlier 'indus' substring search was flooded with false positives from 'industries') and found a genuine 2022 excavator-matching question, image-verified against the answer key. Web research added the standard all-India-reference-book facts (Mound of the Dead, Great Bath, economy, river, excavators, horse evidence) plus a deliberate correction: a coaching-aggregator MCQ claimed Kalibangan's defining feature was 'pottery', but cross-checking multiple reference sites (vajiramandravi, cultureandheritage.org) confirms its actual well-established claim to fame is fire altars and the world's earliest known ploughed field — the aggregator's question likely just didn't offer that as an option. Used the correct fact instead of propagating the aggregator's answer.

reading.intro: Opening topic of Unit III. Real exam evidence shows two recurring traps: assuming ALL Harappan sites were large urban centres (false — many were small villages), and assuming all major Harappan sites are in India (false — Mohenjodaro and Harappa itself are in present-day Pakistan).

qa.question: Real exam: True or false — 'All Harappan sites are urban in character.'
qa.answer: FALSE. While the major cities (Mohenjodaro, Harappa, Dholavira) were large urban centres, Harappan-culture remains have been found even at small village sites — the civilization depended on villages for food and labour, not just cities.

qa.question: Real exam: Which major Harappan sites are NOT located in India?
qa.answer: Mohenjodaro and Harappa — both are in present-day Pakistan (Sindh and Punjab provinces respectively). Lothal, Kalibangan, Bhanwali, Ropar, Surkotada, and Dholavira are all in India.

qa.question: What is the most distinctive feature of Lothal?
qa.answer: Its dockyard — Lothal (in Gujarat) had a sophisticated dockyard, evidence of the Harappans' maritime/trading capability.

qa.question: Who discovered the connection between Harappa and Mohenjodaro, establishing them as one civilization, and when?
qa.answer: Sir John Marshall, Director General of the Archaeological Survey of India, in 1924 — he found common features between the two sites and concluded they were part of one large civilization.

qa.question: What were the Indus Valley Civilization's most advanced urban planning features?
qa.answer: Baked brick houses, elaborate drainage and sewage systems (among the world's earliest known urban sanitation systems — more advanced than contemporary Middle Eastern cities), and planned water supply.

qa.question: What famous bronze artifact was found at Mohenjodaro, and what were the Harappan standard weights' ratio?
qa.answer: The 'Dancing Girl' bronze figurine. Standard cubical weights were found in the ratio 1:2:4:8:16:32.

qa.question: Name the five major Harappan urban centres and their general regions.
qa.answer: Mohenjodaro (lower Indus, Sindh), Harappa (western Punjab), Ganeriwala (Cholistan desert), Dholavira (Gujarat), and Rakhigarhi (Haryana).

qa.question: Real exam (2022_q1 page 8 Q108): Match the excavation sites with their excavators — Chanhudharo, Kulli, Rakhigarhi, Dholavira with Aurel Stein, J.P. Joshi, Majumdar, Amrendranath.
qa.answer: Chanhudharo = Majumdar, Kulli = Aurel Stein, Rakhigarhi = Amrendranath, Dholavira = J.P. Joshi (the exam's marked correct option was '3,1,4,2' in that List-I order).
qa.tip: When a matching question gives 4 sites and 4 excavators, don't try to memorize all pairs at once — anchor the one or two you're SURE of first (e.g. Dholavira is well-known for its water conservation, often paired with J.P. Joshi in reference books) and use elimination for the rest.

qa.question: What does the name 'Mohenjo-daro' literally mean in Sindhi, and what is the site's most famous public structure?
qa.answer: 'Mound of the Dead.' Its most famous public structure is the Great Bath — a large, carefully waterproofed rectangular tank, believed to have been used for ritual/religious bathing.

qa.question: What materials were Harappan tools and weapons made from, and what metal were they notably WITHOUT?
qa.answer: Stone, bronze, and copper — the Indus Valley was a Bronze Age civilization. Notably, they did NOT have iron; iron technology only arrived in India later, in the subsequent Vedic/Iron Age period.

qa.question: What was the backbone of the Indus Valley economy, and what crop were Harappans among the world's earliest cultivators of?
qa.answer: Agriculture was the backbone of the economy (supplemented by extensive internal and maritime trade). The Harappans are credited as among the world's earliest cultivators and users of cotton — the ancient Greeks later derived their word for cotton cloth ('sindon') from the Indus/Sindh region.

qa.question: On which river is Harappa located, and who excavated the site, in what year?
qa.answer: Harappa lies on the river Ravi (in present-day Punjab, Pakistan). It was excavated by Daya Ram Sahni in 1921 — a year before R.D. Banerji's 1922 excavation of Mohenjodaro.

qa.question: What is Kalibangan's genuine claim to fame among Harappan sites (not to be confused with a coaching-site trap answer of 'pottery')?
qa.answer: Kalibangan (on the Ghaggar river, Rajasthan) is famous for two unique finds: the world's earliest known ploughed agricultural field (with criss-cross furrow marks still preserved), and rows of fire altars — suggesting a distinct fire-worship ritual practice not seen at most other Harappan sites.
qa.tip: If you see an MCQ claiming Kalibangan's fame is merely 'pottery' with no fire-altar/ploughed-field option listed, treat it with suspicion — the two genuinely distinctive Kalibangan facts are fire altars and the ploughed field.

qa.question: At which Harappan site was skeletal evidence of horses reportedly found, and why is this significant?
qa.answer: Surkotada (Gujarat) — bone remains identified as horse remains were reported here, which is notable/debated because horses are NOT commonly associated with mainstream Harappan urban life or art (no horse figurines/seals), unlike later Vedic-era culture.

qa.question: What material were Harappan houses typically built from, and how did this compare with contemporary Mesopotamian cities?
qa.answer: Burnt (baked) bricks — a notably more durable, weather-resistant choice than the sun-dried mud bricks commonly used in contemporary Mesopotamian cities, reflecting the Harappans' advanced building technology.

questions.prompt: Which statement about Harappan culture is INCORRECT?
options: All Harappan sites are urban in character
Harappan cities depended on villages for food and labourers
Harappan remains have been found even at small village sites
Lothal's most distinctive feature is its dockyard
questions.explanation: Not all Harappan sites were urban — many were small villages.

questions.prompt: Which of the following major Harappan sites are located in present-day Pakistan, not India?
questions.explanation: Mohenjodaro and Harappa are both in Pakistan.

questions.prompt: Which Harappan site is best known for its dockyard?
Who established that Harappa and Mohenjodaro belonged to the same civilization, in 1924?
Harappan standard cubical weights were found in which ratio?
The famous 'Dancing Girl' bronze figurine was found at:
Real exam (2022_q1 Q108): Which excavator is correctly matched to Chanhudharo?
questions.explanation: Chanhudharo was excavated by Majumdar (the full matched set: Chanhudharo=Majumdar, Kulli=Aurel Stein, Rakhigarhi=Amrendranath, Dholavira=J.P. Joshi).
questions.trick: Anchor the one pairing you're most confident about first, then use elimination for the rest in a 4x4 matching question.

questions.prompt: The Great Bath, a large ritual bathing structure, was located at which Harappan site?
Which metal was notably ABSENT from Harappan tools and weapons, which were instead made of stone, bronze, and copper?
questions.explanation: Iron — the Indus Valley was a Bronze Age civilization; iron technology arrived only in the later Vedic/Iron Age.

questions.prompt: What was the backbone of the Indus Valley Civilization's economy?
Harappa, excavated by Daya Ram Sahni in 1921, is located on the banks of which river?
Which Harappan site is genuinely famous for the world's earliest known ploughed field and rows of fire altars?
questions.trick: Don't confuse Kalibangan's real claim to fame (fire altars + ploughed field) with a poorly-worded distractor answer like 'pottery' — always sanity-check against what the site is genuinely known for.

questions.prompt: Skeletal evidence identified as horse remains, notable since horses are otherwise rare in Harappan art/artifacts, was reported at:
Harappan houses were typically built using which material, more durable than the sun-dried bricks common in contemporary Mesopotamia?
Which Harappan site was excavated by R.D. Banerji in 1922, one year after Daya Ram Sahni excavated Harappa?
